seoTitle: Microchip Products | Microcontrollers, Analog, FPGA, Connectivity | Distributor
seoDescription: Complete catalog of Microchip microcontrollers (PIC, AVR, SAM), analog products, FPGA solutions, and connectivity modules. Technical specifications and selection guide.

faqs.question: What types of microcontrollers does Microchip offer?
faqs.answer: Microchip offers a comprehensive range of microcontrollers across three main architectures: 1) PIC microcontrollers (8-bit and 16-bit) featuring rich analog integration, low power consumption, and extensive peripheral sets ideal for industrial and automotive applications; 2) AVR microcontrollers (8-bit and 32-bit) known for high code efficiency, fast execution, and popularity in maker communities with Arduino compatibility; 3) SAM microcontrollers (32-bit ARM Cortex-M based) providing high-performance computing for complex IoT, industrial automation, and connectivity applications. Each family offers multiple variants with different memory sizes, package options, and specialized features for specific applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our microcontroller categories to explore the complete portfolio, or contact our FAE team for architecture selection guidance based on your application requirements.

faqs.question: How do I select the right Microchip product for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right Microchip product requires evaluating several key factors: 1) Performance requirements - determine if you need 8-bit simplicity, 16-bit enhanced performance, or 32-bit ARM capabilities; 2) Memory needs - calculate required Flash for program storage and RAM for data processing; 3) Peripheral integration - identify required analog channels, communication interfaces (UART, SPI, I2C, CAN, USB), and specialized functions like motor control PWMs; 4) Power constraints - consider sleep mode currents and operating voltage range for battery-powered designs; 5) Connectivity requirements - evaluate wired (Ethernet, USB) or wireless (WiFi, Bluetooth, LoRa) needs; 6) Security features - assess need for hardware crypto, secure boot, or tamper detection for IoT applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our product selection guides or contact our FAE team with your technical specifications for personalized recommendations.

faqs.question: What analog products are available from Microchip?
faqs.answer: Microchip provides a comprehensive portfolio of analog and power management solutions: 1) Operational amplifiers including precision, low-power, and high-speed variants for signal conditioning; 2) Data converters (ADCs and DACs) with resolutions from 8-bit to 24-bit for precision measurement and control; 3) Voltage regulators including LDOs, switching regulators, and voltage references for power management; 4) Battery management ICs for charging, fuel gauging, and protection of Li-ion and other chemistries; 5) Motor drivers for brushed DC, brushless DC, and stepper motors; 6) LED drivers for lighting and display applications; 7) Interface products including CAN, LIN, and RS-485 transceivers for industrial communications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Explore our Analog Products category to find amplifiers, data converters, power management, and interface solutions for your design.

faqs.question: What are the advantages of Microchip's FPGA solutions?
faqs.answer: Microchip's FPGA portfolio offers unique advantages that differentiate them from competitors: 1) Flash-based architecture provides instant-on capability and immunity to single-event upsets (SEU), critical for aerospace and defense applications; 2) Industry-leading low power consumption, essential for battery-powered and thermally constrained designs; 3) Non-volatile configuration eliminates need for external configuration memory and enables secure boot; 4) Comprehensive security features including anti-tamper, DPA-resistant crypto accelerators, and physically unclonable functions (PUF); 5) PolarFire family offers cost-effective solutions with 50% lower power than competing SRAM FPGAs; 6) Extended temperature ranges (-55°C to +125°C) for harsh environment applications; 7) Long product lifecycles (20+ years) ensuring supply continuity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Visit our FPGA category to explore PolarFire, IGLOO, and SmartFusion solutions for your programmable logic requirements.

faqs.question: Does Microchip offer development tools and software support?
faqs.answer: Microchip provides comprehensive development ecosystem support: 1) MPLAB X IDE - free, cross-platform integrated development environment supporting all Microchip microcontrollers with advanced debugging capabilities; 2) MPLAB Harmony - modular firmware development framework for 32-bit SAM and PIC32 applications; 3) Atmel Studio / Microchip Studio - development environment for AVR and SAM microcontrollers; 4) Libero SoC - FPGA design suite for Microchip programmable logic devices; 5) Extensive code libraries and application examples for rapid development; 6) In-circuit debuggers and programmers including PICkit, ICD, and Atmel-ICE; 7) Development boards and evaluation kits for all major product families; 8) Technical documentation including datasheets, application notes, and reference designs available through our distributor portal.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for development tool recommendations and access to software libraries for your Microchip-based designs.

categories.name: Microcontrollers
categories.description: 8-bit, 16-bit, and 32-bit microcontrollers including PIC, AVR, and SAM families for embedded control applications.
categories.longDescription: Microchip's microcontroller portfolio offers industry-leading solutions for embedded control applications, available through authorized distributor BeiLuo. The PIC microcontroller family includes 8-bit PIC16/PIC18 and 16-bit PIC24/dsPIC33 devices featuring rich analog integration, low power consumption, and extensive peripheral sets. The AVR family provides high code efficiency and fast execution popular in maker communities. The SAM family delivers 32-bit ARM Cortex-M performance for complex IoT and industrial applications. All families offer multiple memory options, package variants, and specialized features including motor control, digital power, and connectivity. Our comprehensive selection guide helps you choose the optimal architecture based on performance, power, and cost requirements.
categories.selectionGuide: Select PIC for cost-sensitive applications with analog integration, AVR for code efficiency, SAM for 32-bit performance and connectivity.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PIC and AVR microcontrollers?
faqs.answer: PIC and AVR microcontrollers have distinct architectural differences: PIC uses a modified Harvard architecture with single-cycle execution for most instructions, offering deterministic timing critical for control applications. PIC devices excel in analog integration with advanced ADCs, comparators, and op-amps on-chip. AVR uses a more traditional RISC architecture with 32 general-purpose registers enabling efficient C code compilation and faster context switching. AVR devices typically offer higher MIPS per MHz and are popular for Arduino-compatible designs. PIC has a longer history in industrial applications with proven reliability, while AVR dominates in consumer and educational markets. Both families offer excellent development tools, but PIC uses MPLAB X while AVR traditionally used Atmel Studio (now Microchip Studio).
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose PIC for industrial applications requiring analog integration and deterministic timing; select AVR for code efficiency and rapid prototyping.

faqs.question: How do I choose between 8-bit and 32-bit microcontrollers?
faqs.answer: The choice between 8-bit and 32-bit microcontrollers depends on application complexity and performance requirements: 8-bit MCUs (PIC16/18, AVR) are ideal for simple control tasks, sensor interfaces, and cost-sensitive applications requiring minimal processing power. They offer lower power consumption, smaller packages, and reduced BOM cost. 32-bit MCUs (SAM, PIC32) are required for complex algorithms, graphics processing, protocol stacks, and RTOS-based designs. They provide significantly more memory, higher clock speeds, and advanced peripherals like Ethernet and USB. Consider 8-bit when your application involves simple I/O control, basic timing, and low-cost requirements. Choose 32-bit when you need DSP capabilities, extensive connectivity, floating-point math, or plan to run an operating system.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with 8-bit for simple applications under $2 BOM cost; choose 32-bit for complex processing, connectivity, or when memory needs exceed 128KB Flash.

faqs.question: What development tools do I need for Microchip microcontrollers?
faqs.answer: Microchip provides comprehensive free development tools: MPLAB X IDE is the primary development environment supporting all PIC, AVR, and SAM microcontrollers with integrated compiler, debugger, and programmer support. The XC8, XC16, and XC32 compilers provide optimized C/C++ compilation for respective architectures. For debugging, you'll need hardware tools like PICkit 4 (entry-level), ICD 4 (professional), or Atmel-ICE (for AVR/SAM). Development boards like Curiosity Nano, Xplained Pro, and Explorer boards provide low-cost evaluation platforms. MPLAB Harmony framework accelerates 32-bit development with middleware and drivers. All tools are available as free downloads, with optional paid Pro compiler optimizations. As an authorized distributor, BeiLuo can provide bundled development kits at competitive pricing.
faqs.decisionGuide: Download MPLAB X IDE and the appropriate XC compiler for free. Contact us for hardware debugger and development board recommendations based on your target MCU family.

faqs.question: What is the typical product lifecycle for Microchip microcontrollers?
faqs.answer: Microchip is renowned for industry-leading product lifecycles, typically guaranteeing 15-20 years of availability for most microcontroller families. This long-term supply commitment is critical for industrial, automotive, and medical applications requiring extended production runs. Microchip's Product Longevity Program (PLP) formally commits to maintaining production for specific devices, with some families guaranteed for 25+ years. Unlike competitors who obsolete products after 5-7 years, Microchip understands that embedded designs often require decade-long support. When a product does reach end-of-life, Microchip provides 12-24 months advance notice and typically offers pin-compatible migration paths to newer devices. This supply stability significantly reduces redesign costs and qualification efforts for long-life products.
faqs.decisionGuide: For applications requiring 10+ year production lifecycles, Microchip MCUs are an excellent choice. Contact our FAE team for specific product longevity commitments.

faqs.question: Do Microchip microcontrollers support functional safety applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, Microchip offers extensive functional safety support for ISO 26262 (automotive) and IEC 61508 (industrial) applications. Many SAM and PIC32 devices are available with Safety Manuals providing FMEDA (Failure Modes, Effects, and Diagnostics Analysis) data for ASIL-B/D compliance. The dsPIC33C family includes specialized Digital Signal Controllers with hardware safety features like dual-core lockstep, ECC memory, and clock monitoring. Microchip provides safety-certified software libraries and RTOS options. For development, MPLAB X supports code coverage analysis and static analysis integration. As an authorized distributor, BeiLuo can provide safety documentation, reference designs for safety-critical applications, and access to Microchip's functional safety experts for architecture guidance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our automotive/industrial FAE specialists for functional safety documentation and MCU recommendations for your safety-critical application.

products.name: PIC18F46K22 Enhanced Flash Microcontroller
products.shortDescription: High-performance 8-bit MCU with 64KB Flash, rich analog peripherals, and XLP technology for low-power applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The PIC18F46K22 is a high-performance enhanced flash microcontroller featuring 64KB of program memory and 3896 bytes of RAM. It operates at speeds up to 64MHz with XLP (eXtreme Low Power) technology consuming as little as 34μA/MHz in active mode.

This device integrates comprehensive analog peripherals including a 12-bit ADC with up to 30 channels, two comparators, and a programmable voltage reference. The rich digital peripheral set includes two enhanced USARTs, two SPI/I2C modules, and enhanced PWM capabilities.

With industrial temperature range (-40°C to +85°C) and robust ESD protection, the PIC18F46K22 is ideal for industrial control, automotive body electronics, and battery-powered IoT applications requiring long-term availability.
features: XLP technology for ultra-low power consumption
Two enhanced USART with LIN support

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Embedded Systems
faeReview.content: The PIC18F46K22 is one of my go-to recommendations for industrial customers needing a robust 8-bit solution. The XLP technology really delivers on power savings - I've seen battery life extended by 40% compared to standard PIC18 devices in sensor applications. The 12-bit ADC with computation module is a standout feature that offloads filtering and averaging from the CPU, freeing up cycles for control algorithms. I particularly like the CTMU for capacitive touch applications, eliminating the need for external touch controllers. The LIN-capable USARTs make this perfect for automotive body electronics. My only caution is that the 1.8V operation requires careful power supply design - ensure your voltage regulator has good transient response. Overall, an excellent workhorse MCU for cost-sensitive industrial applications.
faeReview.highlight: XLP technology delivers 40% power savings; 12-bit ADC with computation offloads CPU

alternativeParts.reason: Newer generation with enhanced ADC computation and security features
alternativeParts.useCase: Upgrade path for designs requiring improved security or ADC performance

alternativeParts.reason: AVR architecture alternative with similar memory, popular in maker community
alternativeParts.useCase: Alternative for teams familiar with AVR/Arduino ecosystem

companionParts.description: Low-quiescent current LDO regulator, ideal for battery-powered designs
General-purpose op-amp for signal conditioning applications
Standalone CAN controller for automotive/industrial networks
Smaller pin-count variant (28-pin) for cost-sensitive designs

faqs.question: What is the maximum clock frequency for PIC18F46K22?
faqs.answer: The PIC18F46K22 supports maximum clock frequencies up to 64MHz using the internal oscillator with PLL. When using external crystal oscillators, the maximum frequency depends on the oscillator mode: HS mode supports up to 25MHz crystals, while HSPLL mode can multiply a 4-10MHz crystal up to 40MHz. The device also supports external clock inputs up to 64MHz. For power-sensitive applications, the clock can be scaled down to 31kHz using the internal LFINTOSC. The flexible clock system allows dynamic clock switching during operation to optimize power consumption versus performance based on application requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 64MHz for maximum performance; consider lower frequencies or sleep modes for power-sensitive battery applications.

faqs.question: How much current does PIC18F46K22 consume in sleep mode?
faqs.answer: The PIC18F46K22 features industry-leading low power consumption thanks to XLP technology. Typical sleep mode current is just 20nA when using the LFINTOSC with all peripherals disabled. With the watchdog timer enabled, sleep current increases to approximately 300nA. In active mode, current consumption is 34μA/MHz typical at 3.3V. For battery-powered applications, the device supports multiple idle and sleep modes with selective peripheral operation - for example, you can maintain Timer1 and the ADC active while the CPU sleeps, consuming only 1.2μA while still sampling sensors. These ultra-low power modes make the PIC18F46K22 ideal for energy harvesting and long-life battery applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for 20nA sleep current by disabling all peripherals; use selective wake-on-interrupt for battery-powered sensor nodes.

faqs.question: Can PIC18F46K22 operate at 3.3V and interface with 5V devices?
faqs.answer: Yes, the PIC18F46K22 operates over a wide voltage range from 1.8V to 5.5V, making it compatible with both 3.3V and 5V systems. At 3.3V operation, all GPIO pins are 5V-tolerant on input, meaning you can safely connect 5V logic signals directly to the inputs without damage. For outputs driving 5V logic, the high-level output voltage (VOH) at 3.3V supply is approximately 3.0V, which is above the 2.0V minimum required by standard 5V CMOS logic. However, for 5V TTL logic, you may need level translators for reliable high-level recognition. Microchip offers companion level translator devices like the PCA9306 for I2C bus voltage translation between 3.3V and 5V domains.
faqs.decisionGuide: Inputs are 5V-tolerant at 3.3V operation; use level translators for 5V outputs requiring full 5V swing.

faqs.question: What is the ADC computation module and how does it help?
faqs.answer: The PIC18F46K22 features an advanced ADC2 (Analog-to-Digital Converter with Computation) module that offloads common signal processing tasks from the CPU. The computation module can automatically perform averaging, accumulation, low-pass filtering, and threshold comparison without CPU intervention. For example, you can configure the ADC to take 16 samples, average them, and compare against a threshold - all while the CPU remains in sleep mode. This reduces power consumption and frees the CPU for other tasks. The module supports oversampling for enhanced resolution (up to 14-bit effective from 12-bit hardware) and can trigger interrupts only when significant changes occur, further reducing power and processing overhead.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ADC computation for sensor averaging and filtering to reduce CPU load and enable longer sleep periods in battery applications.

faqs.question: How do I program and debug PIC18F46K22?
faqs.answer: The PIC18F46K22 is programmed and debugged using Microchip's standard ICSP (In-Circuit Serial Programming) interface, requiring only five connections: VPP/MCLR (programming voltage), ICSPCLK (clock), ICSPDAT (data), VDD, and VSS. Compatible programmers include the low-cost PICkit 4 (PG164140), the professional-grade MPLAB ICD 4 (DV164045), and the high-end MPLAB REAL ICE. For development, the Curiosity High Pin Count board (DM164136) provides an integrated programmer/debugger with USB connection. The free MPLAB X IDE supports full debugging capabilities including breakpoints, single-stepping, variable watch windows, and real-time data monitoring. Production programming services are available through BeiLuo for volume orders.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use PICkit 4 for development and low-volume programming; contact us for production programming services on volume orders.

products.name: SAM D21 ARM Cortex-M0+ Microcontroller
products.shortDescription: Ultra-low power 32-bit ARM Cortex-M0+ MCU with 256KB Flash, USB, and rich peripheral set for IoT applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The ATSAMD21G18A is a high-performance 32-bit microcontroller based on the ARM Cortex-M0+ processor running at up to 48MHz. It features 256KB of Flash memory and 32KB of SRAM, providing ample resources for complex IoT and embedded applications.

This device integrates a full-speed USB 2.0 device and embedded host controller, making it ideal for USB-connected applications. The rich peripheral set includes six flexible serial communication modules (SERCOM) configurable as UART, SPI, or I2C, 12-channel DMA, and a 12-bit ADC with up to 20 channels.

With ultra-low power consumption down to 70μA/MHz in active mode and sub-2μA in standby with RTC, the SAM D21 is optimized for battery-powered IoT devices. The device is supported by the comprehensive Atmel START and MPLAB Harmony development frameworks.

faeReview.author: Sarah Johnson
faeReview.title: Principal FAE - IoT and Connectivity
faeReview.content: The SAM D21 is my top recommendation for IoT gateway and USB device applications. The six SERCOM modules are incredibly flexible - you can configure them as UART, SPI, or I2C on the fly, which is a lifesaver when board layouts need last-minute changes. The USB peripheral is rock-solid and supports both device and host modes, perfect for USB-to-serial bridges or USB-powered sensor hubs. I've measured active current at 75μA/MHz typical, which is excellent for a 32-bit MCU. The touch controller (PTC) works well for capacitive buttons without external components. One limitation is the 48MHz max clock - for DSP-heavy applications, consider the SAM D51 series instead. The extensive HAL support in both Atmel START and Arduino makes development very fast.
faeReview.highlight: Flexible SERCOM modules enable last-minute interface changes; excellent USB peripheral for device/host applications

alternativeParts.reason: Higher performance Cortex-M4F with DSP and FPU for compute-intensive applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Upgrade path for applications requiring DSP, floating-point, or higher processing power

alternativeParts.reason: Alternative ultra-low power Cortex-M0+ from STM32 ecosystem
alternativeParts.useCase: Alternative for teams with existing STM32 codebase or tools

companionParts.description: WiFi and Bluetooth 4.0 module for IoT connectivity

faqs.question: What is the difference between SAM D21 and SAM D51?
faqs.answer: The SAM D21 and SAM D51 are both ARM-based microcontrollers but target different performance levels. The SAM D21 uses a Cortex-M0+ core running at 48MHz, ideal for low-power IoT applications with basic processing needs. It offers excellent power efficiency at 70μA/MHz but lacks DSP capabilities. The SAM D51 uses a Cortex-M4F core running at 120MHz with hardware floating-point unit (FPU) and DSP instructions, delivering significantly higher compute performance for complex algorithms, motor control, and graphics. The D51 also provides more memory (up to 1MB Flash vs 256KB) and advanced security features. Choose D21 for cost-sensitive, low-power IoT; choose D51 for performance-intensive applications requiring DSP or floating-point math.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select SAM D21 for basic IoT and USB applications under $3; choose SAM D51 for DSP, motor control, or graphics requiring >48MHz performance.

faqs.question: How do I configure SERCOM modules for different interfaces?
faqs.answer: The SAM D21 features six SERCOM (Serial Communication) modules that can be individually configured as UART, SPI, or I2C through the PORT and SERCOM registers. Configuration is done via the Atmel START code configurator or by directly writing registers. Each SERCOM uses specific pin mappings defined in the datasheet - for example, SERCOM0 can use PA08/PA09 for I2C or PA04-PA07 for SPI. The flexibility allows you to assign interfaces based on PCB layout constraints rather than fixed pinouts. To configure: 1) Enable SERCOM clock in GCLK and PM; 2) Configure PORT pins for the desired function; 3) Write SERCOM CTRLA register to select mode (USART, SPI, or I2C); 4) Set baud rate and frame format in CTRLB and BAUD registers; 5) Enable the peripheral. The MPLAB Harmony and Arduino cores abstract this configuration for rapid development.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Atmel START for graphical pin configuration; assign SERCOMs based on PCB routing constraints for optimal layout.

faqs.question: What development boards are available for SAM D21?
faqs.answer: Microchip offers several development boards for SAM D21 evaluation: 1) SAM D21 Xplained Pro (ATSAMD21-XPRO) - professional evaluation board with embedded debugger, Arduino-compatible headers, and multiple sensors; 2) Curiosity Nano Evaluation Kit (DM320119) - low-cost board with integrated debugger in a compact form factor; 3) Arduino Zero - Arduino-compatible board using SAM D21, ideal for makers and rapid prototyping; 4) Adafruit Feather M0 - popular third-party board with LiPo battery charging and Feather ecosystem compatibility. All boards include USB debugging and programming capability. The Xplained Pro is recommended for comprehensive evaluation with its rich peripheral expansion options, while the Curiosity Nano offers the best value for basic development.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose SAM D21 Xplained Pro for comprehensive evaluation; Curiosity Nano for cost-sensitive projects; Arduino Zero for maker community compatibility.

faqs.question: Can SAM D21 run Arduino sketches?
faqs.answer: Yes, the SAM D21 is fully supported by the Arduino ecosystem. The Arduino Zero board uses the ATSAMD21G18A microcontroller, and Microchip provides official Arduino core support for the SAM D21 family. This means you can use the familiar Arduino IDE and libraries for rapid prototyping. The Arduino core abstracts the low-level register configuration, providing easy-to-use functions for GPIO, ADC, PWM, I2C, SPI, and USB. Advanced features like DMA and the event system are also accessible through Arduino libraries. For production development, you can migrate from Arduino to bare-metal programming or use MPLAB X with Harmony framework while retaining the same hardware. The extensive Arduino library ecosystem significantly accelerates development for common IoT sensors and wireless modules.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Arduino for rapid prototyping and proof-of-concept; migrate to MPLAB X for production firmware requiring optimization.

faqs.question: What is the power consumption of SAM D21 in different modes?
faqs.answer: The SAM D21 offers excellent power efficiency across multiple operating modes: Active mode consumes approximately 70μA/MHz at 3.3V when executing from Flash - so at 48MHz, expect around 3.4mA. Idle mode (CPU stopped, peripherals running) reduces consumption to about 1.2mA. Standby mode with RTC and SRAM retention consumes only 2.5μA typical, making it ideal for battery-powered sensor nodes that wake periodically. Deep sleep mode without RTC drops to 0.5μA but requires external reset to wake. The device supports multiple clock domains allowing selective peripheral gating - for example, you can run the ADC from a 32kHz clock while the CPU sleeps. The event system enables peripheral-to-peripheral communication without CPU wakeups, further reducing average power consumption in event-driven applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use standby with RTC for periodic sensor sampling achieving 2.5μA average; use event system to minimize CPU wakeups.

products.name: ATmega328P 8-bit AVR Microcontroller
products.shortDescription: Popular 8-bit AVR MCU with 32KB Flash, 2KB SRAM, and 1KB EEPROM. Widely used in Arduino Uno and educational projects.
descriptionParagraphs: The ATmega328P is one of the most popular 8-bit microcontrollers, best known as the heart of the Arduino Uno. It features 32KB of in-system programmable Flash, 2KB of SRAM, and 1KB of EEPROM.

Operating at up to 20MHz, this MCU provides sufficient performance for a wide range of embedded applications. The rich peripheral set includes 23 GPIO pins, 6-channel 10-bit ADC, UART, SPI, and I2C interfaces.
With its low power consumption and wide operating voltage range (1.8V-5.5V), the ATmega328P is ideal for battery-powered applications, educational projects, and cost-sensitive designs.

faeReview.author: David Lee
faeReview.title: FAE - Educational and Maker Markets
faeReview.content: The ATmega328P is the workhorse of the maker community. Its popularity stems from the Arduino ecosystem, but it's a capable MCU in its own right. I recommend it for educational projects, simple control applications, and when Arduino compatibility is needed. The DIP package is perfect for breadboarding and learning. For production designs, consider the TQFP or QFN packages for smaller footprint.
faeReview.highlight: Arduino-compatible with excellent educational resources and community support

alternativeParts.reason: More memory and higher performance for complex applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Applications requiring more resources than ATmega328P

companionParts.description: Low-dropout voltage regulator for 5V to 3.3V conversion

faqs.question: What is the difference between ATmega328 and ATmega328P?
faqs.answer: The ATmega328P is the picoPower version of the ATmega328. The 'P' indicates lower power consumption through additional power-saving features like the PRR (Power Reduction Register) which allows disabling individual peripherals. The ATmega328P also has a wider operating voltage range (1.8V-5.5V vs 2.7V-5.5V). Both are code-compatible, but the 328P is recommended for all new designs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always use ATmega328P for new designs - lower power and wider voltage range at the same price.

faqs.question: Can I use Arduino code on ATmega328P without Arduino board?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can program the ATmega328P directly using the Arduino IDE and a programmer like USBasp or Arduino as ISP. Select 'ATmega328P' as the board and use 'Upload Using Programmer' option. You'll need to configure the fuses for the desired clock source (internal 8MHz or external crystal). The Arduino bootloader is not required for this approach, saving 2KB of Flash memory.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use direct programming to save bootloader space and reduce cost for production designs.

products.name: PIC16F877A Enhanced Flash Microcontroller
products.shortDescription: Classic 8-bit PIC microcontroller with 14KB Flash, 368 bytes RAM, and extensive peripheral set for industrial applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The PIC16F877A is a classic 8-bit microcontroller that has been widely used in industrial and educational applications for over two decades. It features 14KB of Flash program memory and 368 bytes of data memory.

This device includes a rich set of peripherals including 33 GPIO pins, 8-channel 10-bit ADC, UART, SPI, and I2C interfaces. The built-in EEPROM (256 bytes) provides non-volatile storage for configuration data.
With its robust design, wide availability, and extensive documentation, the PIC16F877A remains a popular choice for industrial control systems, educational projects, and legacy product support.

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Embedded Systems
faeReview.content: The PIC16F877A is a classic workhorse that has stood the test of time. While newer MCUs offer more features, the 877A remains relevant for educational purposes and legacy system maintenance. I often recommend it for students learning embedded systems due to its straightforward architecture and extensive learning resources. For new industrial designs, consider the PIC18F series for better performance.
faeReview.highlight: Classic PIC architecture with extensive documentation and educational resources

alternativeParts.reason: Significantly more resources and performance for modern applications
alternativeParts.useCase: New designs requiring more memory and performance

faqs.question: Is the PIC16F877A still recommended for new designs?
faqs.answer: While the PIC16F877A is still available and supported, Microchip recommends newer devices like the PIC18F46K22 or PIC16F18877 for new designs. These newer devices offer significantly more Flash, RAM, and performance at similar or lower cost. The 877A remains suitable for educational purposes, legacy product maintenance, and when specific peripherals or package options are required.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use PIC18F46K22 for new designs; use 877A for education or legacy maintenance only.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PIC16F877 and PIC16F877A?
faqs.answer: The PIC16F877A is an enhanced version of the PIC16F877 with several improvements: 1) Faster Flash write operations; 2) Lower power consumption in sleep mode; 3) Enhanced oscillator options; 4) Additional brown-out reset options. The 'A' version is pin-compatible and code-compatible with the original 877, making it a drop-in replacement. Always use the 877A version for new purchases.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always specify PIC16F877A - improved performance at the same cost.

categories.name: Analog Products
categories.description: Operational amplifiers, comparators, voltage references, and analog interface products for signal conditioning and precision measurement.
categories.longDescription: Microchip's analog product portfolio includes high-performance operational amplifiers, precision voltage references, analog comparators, and analog switches. These products enable accurate signal conditioning, sensor interface, and precision measurement applications.
categories.selectionGuide: Select based on bandwidth, precision, and power requirements for your signal conditioning needs.

faqs.question: How do I select the right op amp for my application?
faqs.answer: Consider these key parameters: 1) Gain Bandwidth Product (GBP) - must be 10x your signal bandwidth; 2) Input offset voltage - critical for precision DC applications; 3) Supply voltage range - must match your system voltage; 4) Power consumption - important for battery applications; 5) Package size - consider PCB space constraints.

products.name: MCP6002 Dual General Purpose Op Amp
products.shortDescription: Dual rail-to-rail input/output op amp with 1MHz gain bandwidth, low power consumption, and wide supply range.
descriptionParagraphs: The MCP6002 is a dual general-purpose operational amplifier featuring rail-to-rail input and output operation. With 1MHz gain bandwidth product and low power consumption of 100μA per amplifier, it's ideal for battery-powered applications.
The device operates from a single supply voltage of 1.8V to 6.0V, making it compatible with a wide range of systems. The rail-to-rail output swing maximizes dynamic range in low-voltage applications.
Available in standard 8-pin packages, the MCP6002 is pin-compatible with many industry-standard dual op amps, allowing easy substitution in existing designs.

faeReview.author: Sarah Johnson
faeReview.title: FAE - Analog Products
faeReview.content: The MCP6002 is my go-to recommendation for general-purpose op amp applications. The rail-to-rail operation is essential for modern low-voltage designs, and the low power consumption makes it perfect for battery-powered devices. I've used it successfully in sensor conditioning circuits, audio preamps, and active filters. The 1MHz bandwidth is sufficient for most sensor applications.
faeReview.highlight: Rail-to-rail operation with low power consumption for battery applications

faqs.question: What is the maximum output current of MCP6002?
faqs.answer: The MCP6002 can source or sink approximately 23mA of output current. For applications requiring higher drive capability, consider the MCP6001/2/4 series with higher output current or add an external transistor buffer stage.
faqs.decisionGuide: For loads >20mA, add external buffer or select higher drive op amp.

products.name: MCP6001 Single General Purpose Op Amp
products.shortDescription: Single rail-to-rail op amp with 1MHz bandwidth, low power, and SC70 package option for space-constrained designs.
descriptionParagraphs: The MCP6001 is a single-channel version of the popular MCP6002 op amp, offering the same performance in a smaller package. It features rail-to-rail input and output operation with 1MHz gain bandwidth.
Available in tiny SC70-5 and SOT-23-5 packages, the MCP6001 is ideal for space-constrained portable applications. The device consumes only 100μA quiescent current.
The wide 1.8V to 6.0V supply range and industrial temperature rating make it suitable for a broad range of applications.

faeReview.author: Sarah Johnson
faeReview.title: FAE - Analog Products
faeReview.content: The MCP6001 in SC70 package is perfect for wearable and portable devices where space is critical. The performance matches the MCP6002 but in a much smaller footprint. I recommend it for single-channel applications in fitness trackers, smart watches, and IoT sensors.
faeReview.highlight: Tiny SC70 package ideal for space-constrained portable designs

faqs.question: What is the smallest package available for MCP6001?
faqs.answer: The MCP6001 is available in SC70-5 package measuring just 2.0mm x 2.1mm. This makes it one of the smallest op amps available, perfect for wearable and portable applications with severe space constraints.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use SC70-5 for minimum footprint; use SOT-23-5 for easier hand soldering.

products.name: MCP6V02 Zero-Drift Op Amp
products.shortDescription: Precision zero-drift op amp with 5MHz bandwidth, ultra-low offset (±2μV), and auto-calibration for high-precision applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The MCP6V02 is a precision zero-drift operational amplifier featuring an on-chip auto-calibration circuit that eliminates input offset voltage. With typical offset of just ±2μV, it's ideal for precision DC applications.
The 5MHz gain bandwidth product provides higher speed than standard precision op amps while maintaining excellent DC accuracy. The device operates from 1.8V to 5.5V supplies.
Zero-drift architecture continuously corrects offset errors, providing stable performance over temperature and time without manual calibration.

faeReview.author: Dr. James Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Precision Analog
faeReview.content: The MCP6V02 is my recommendation for any precision DC application. The ±2μV offset is exceptional - I've used it in current sense circuits with 10mΩ shunts where offset errors would be unacceptable. The auto-calibration eliminates the need for manual trimming, saving production costs. For high-precision strain gauge and sensor applications, this op amp delivers excellent results.
faeReview.highlight: Ultra-low ±2μV offset with auto-calibration for precision applications

faqs.question: How does zero-drift architecture work?
faqs.answer: Zero-drift op amps use an internal auto-calibration circuit that continuously measures and corrects input offset voltage. The circuit switches between normal operation and calibration phases at high frequency (typically kHz range), storing offset correction in internal capacitors. This provides continuous offset correction without external components, maintaining precision over temperature and time.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use zero-drift op amps for DC precision >12-bit accuracy or when drift over temperature must be minimized.

products.name: MCP3202 12-Bit A/D Converter
products.shortDescription: Dual-channel 12-bit SAR ADC with SPI interface, 100ksps sampling rate, and low power consumption for embedded systems.
descriptionParagraphs: The MCP3202 is a 12-bit successive approximation register (SAR) analog-to-digital converter with two input channels. It features a SPI-compatible serial interface and sampling rates up to 100ksps.
With low power consumption of 400μA active and 500nA standby, the MCP3202 is ideal for battery-powered data acquisition systems. The device operates from a single 2.7V to 5.5V supply.
The differential input capability allows measurement of differential signals or can be configured for single-ended operation. Internal sample-and-hold captures the input signal for conversion.

faeReview.author: Michael Torres
faeReview.title: FAE - Data Conversion
faeReview.content: The MCP3202 provides excellent value for 12-bit data acquisition. The dual channels are useful for differential measurements or monitoring two sensors. The SPI interface is easy to implement with any microcontroller. I typically recommend it for applications requiring 10-12 bit resolution at moderate sampling rates. The low standby current is great for intermittent sampling applications.
faeReview.highlight: Dual-channel 12-bit ADC with low power for embedded data acquisition

faqs.question: What is the maximum SPI clock speed for MCP3202?
faqs.answer: The MCP3202 supports SPI clock rates up to 1.6MHz at 5V operation, or 0.8MHz at 2.7V. At 5V with 1.6MHz clock, a complete 12-bit conversion takes approximately 17 clock cycles, allowing the maximum 100ksps sampling rate. Ensure your microcontroller SPI can support these clock rates for maximum throughput.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 1.6MHz SPI clock at 5V for maximum 100ksps rate; reduce clock at lower voltages.

categories.name: Power Management
categories.description: Voltage regulators, battery management, and power supply solutions for efficient power conversion and battery-powered applications.
categories.longDescription: Microchip's power management portfolio includes low-dropout (LDO) regulators, switching regulators, battery chargers, and voltage supervisors. These products enable efficient power conversion and management for battery-powered and line-powered systems.
categories.selectionGuide: Select based on input/output voltage, current requirements, and efficiency needs for your power supply design.

faqs.question: How do I choose between LDO and switching regulator?
faqs.answer: Use LDO when: input-output voltage difference is small (<1V), low noise is critical, simplicity is preferred, or cost is sensitive. Use switching regulator when: efficiency is critical, input-output difference is large, or battery life must be maximized.

products.name: MCP1703 Low Quiescent Current LDO
products.shortDescription: 250mA LDO with ultra-low 2.0μA quiescent current, high PSRR, and stable with ceramic capacitors for battery applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The MCP1703 is a CMOS low-dropout (LDO) voltage regulator delivering up to 250mA output current with exceptionally low quiescent current of just 2.0μA. It's ideal for extending battery life in portable applications.
The device is stable with ceramic output capacitors as small as 1.0μF, reducing board space and cost. High power supply rejection ratio (PSRR) of 70dB at 1kHz minimizes noise coupling.
Available in fixed output voltages from 1.2V to 5.0V and adjustable versions, the MCP1703 supports a wide range of application requirements.

faeReview.author: Lisa Wang
faeReview.title: FAE - Power Management
faeReview.content: The MCP1703 is my top recommendation for battery-powered designs where every microamp counts. The 2μA quiescent current is exceptional - in a system drawing 10μA average, the regulator only adds 20% overhead. The ceramic cap stability eliminates the need for expensive tantalum capacitors. I've used it in wireless sensor nodes that run for years on coin cell batteries.
faeReview.highlight: Ultra-low 2μA quiescent current extends battery life significantly

faqs.question: What is the dropout voltage of MCP1703?
faqs.answer: The MCP1703 dropout voltage is typically 625mV at 250mA load, 400mV at 150mA, and 200mV at 50mA. At light loads (<1mA), dropout can be as low as 50mV. For minimum dropout operation, keep load current low or consider the MCP1711 with lower dropout voltage.
faqs.decisionGuide: Ensure input voltage exceeds output by at least 1V for reliable regulation at full load.

products.name: MCP1700 Low Quiescent LDO
products.shortDescription: 250mA LDO with 1.6μA quiescent current, low dropout, and SOT-23 package for space-constrained battery applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The MCP1700 is an ultra-low quiescent current LDO regulator delivering 250mA output with just 1.6μA ground current. It's designed specifically for extended battery life in portable applications.
The device features low dropout voltage of 350mV at 250mA and is stable with ceramic output capacitors. Fixed output voltages from 1.2V to 5.0V are available.
The small SOT-23 package minimizes board space while the wide operating temperature range supports industrial applications.

faeReview.author: Lisa Wang
faeReview.title: FAE - Power Management
faeReview.content: The MCP1700 is the lowest quiescent current LDO in Microchip's portfolio at just 1.6μA. It's my first choice for coin cell powered devices where battery life is critical. The SOT-23 package is tiny and the dropout voltage is reasonable for most battery applications. For 3V to 1.8V regulation from a coin cell, this is perfect.
faeReview.highlight: Lowest 1.6μA quiescent current for maximum battery life

faqs.question: What is the difference between MCP1700 and MCP1703?
faqs.answer: The MCP1700 has lower quiescent current (1.6μA vs 2.0μA) and lower dropout voltage (350mV vs 625mV at 250mA), but lower PSRR (44dB vs 70dB). The MCP1700 has lower maximum input voltage (6V vs 13.2V). Choose MCP1700 for maximum battery life; choose MCP1703 for better PSRR or higher input voltage.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use MCP1700 for lowest power; use MCP1703 for better noise rejection or higher input voltage.

products.name: MCP73831 Li-Ion Battery Charger
products.shortDescription: Single-cell Li-Ion/Li-Polymer charge management controller with programmable current, thermal regulation, and tiny SOT-23-5 package.
descriptionParagraphs: The MCP73831 is a fully integrated linear charge management controller for single-cell lithium-ion and lithium-polymer batteries. It provides precise charge control with programmable charge current up to 500mA.

The device features automatic preconditioning of deeply discharged cells, constant-current/constant-voltage charge algorithm, and automatic charge termination. Thermal regulation prevents overheating during charging.
The tiny SOT-23-5 package and minimal external components (just one resistor) make it ideal for space-constrained portable applications.

faeReview.author: Tom Anderson
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Battery Management
faeReview.content: The MCP73831 is my go-to charger for single-cell Li-Ion applications. The programmable current allows optimization of charge time vs. thermal constraints. The tiny SOT-23-5 package is perfect for wearable devices. I appreciate the thermal regulation feature that automatically reduces charge current if the IC gets too hot - this allows maximizing charge current without thermal risk.
faeReview.highlight: Tiny SOT-23-5 package with programmable charge current and thermal regulation

faqs.question: How do I set the charge current for MCP73831?
faqs.answer: The charge current is set by a resistor (RPROG) connected between the PROG pin and ground. The formula is: ICHARGE = 1000V / RPROG. For example, a 2kΩ resistor sets 500mA, 10kΩ sets 100mA. The resistor should be 1% tolerance for accurate current setting. Minimum recommended is 2kΩ (500mA max).
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 2kΩ for 500mA fast charge; use 10kΩ for 100mA slow/thermal-limited charge.

products.name: TC7660 Charge Pump Voltage Converter
products.shortDescription: Switched-capacitor voltage converter that inverts or doubles input voltage, converting +5V to -5V or +10V with few external components.
descriptionParagraphs: The TC7660 is a charge pump voltage converter that can invert or double an input voltage using only two external capacitors. It converts a positive voltage in the +1.5V to +10V range to its negative equivalent (-1.5V to -10V).

The device operates by switching capacitors between series and parallel configurations, eliminating the need for inductors. This provides a compact, low-EMI solution for generating negative supply voltages.
With output currents up to 40mA and conversion efficiency of 99%, the TC7660 is ideal for generating negative rails for op amps, ADCs, and interface circuits in single-supply systems.

faeReview.author: Robert Kim
faeReview.title: FAE - Power Conversion
faeReview.content: The TC7660 is the classic charge pump solution for generating negative voltages. When you need a simple -5V supply for op amps in a single-supply system, this is the easiest solution. No inductors means no EMI headaches and simple PCB layout. The efficiency is excellent for light loads. I use it regularly for RS-232 level shifters and bipolar op amp supplies.
faeReview.highlight: Simple 2-capacitor solution for negative voltage generation without inductors

faqs.question: What capacitor values should I use with TC7660?
faqs.answer: The TC7660 requires two capacitors: a pump capacitor (C1) between pins 2 and 4, and a reservoir capacitor (C2) at the output. Use 10μF electrolytic or ceramic capacitors for most applications. For higher output current, increase C2 to 47μF or 100μF. Use low-ESR capacitors for best performance. The capacitors should be rated for the full input/output voltage.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 10μF for standard applications; increase to 47-100μF for higher current.

categories.name: Connectivity
categories.description: Wireless and wired connectivity solutions including WiFi, Bluetooth, Ethernet, and CAN transceivers for IoT and industrial applications.
categories.longDescription: Microchip's connectivity portfolio includes wireless modules (WiFi, Bluetooth, LoRa), Ethernet controllers, CAN transceivers, and USB solutions. These products enable seamless communication for IoT, industrial, and consumer applications.
categories.selectionGuide: Select based on wireless protocol, data rate, range requirements, and host interface for your connectivity needs.

faqs.question: How do I choose between WiFi and Bluetooth for my IoT application?
faqs.answer: Use WiFi for: high data rates, direct internet connectivity, longer range with infrastructure, video/audio streaming. Use Bluetooth for: low power, short range, direct device-to-device, audio accessories, battery-powered sensors.

products.name: ATWINC3400 WiFi and Bluetooth Module
products.shortDescription: IEEE 802.11 b/g/n WiFi + Bluetooth 4.0 module with integrated TCP/IP stack, AT command interface, and compact surface-mount package.
descriptionParagraphs: The ATWINC3400 is a fully integrated WiFi and Bluetooth module providing IEEE 802.11 b/g/n WiFi connectivity along with Bluetooth 4.0/BLE support. The module includes a complete TCP/IP network stack and security protocols.
Featuring an AT command interface, the module can be controlled by any host microcontroller via UART, SPI, or I2C interfaces. This eliminates the need for complex WiFi driver development.
The compact 26.5mm x 13.5mm surface-mount module includes integrated antenna, crystal, and all RF circuitry, minimizing external components and accelerating time-to-market.

faeReview.author: Jennifer Park
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Wireless Connectivity
faeReview.content: The ATWINC3400 is my top recommendation for adding WiFi to embedded designs. The AT command interface means any microcontroller can control it without complex WiFi drivers. The integrated antenna and certification save months of development time. I've used it in IoT sensors, smart home devices, and industrial monitors with excellent results. The dual Bluetooth capability is a bonus for BLE sensor connectivity.
faeReview.highlight: AT command interface eliminates WiFi driver development; pre-certified module

faqs.question: What host interfaces does ATWINC3400 support?
faqs.answer: The ATWINC3400 supports UART (default), SPI, and I2C host interfaces. UART is the simplest with AT commands. SPI offers higher throughput for data-intensive applications. I2C is available for systems with limited I/O. The interface is selected by strapping pins during power-up.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use UART for simple control; use SPI for high data throughput applications.

products.name: MCP2515 Stand-Alone CAN Controller
products.shortDescription: Stand-alone CAN 2.0B controller with SPI interface, message filtering, and maskable interrupts for automotive and industrial CAN networks.
descriptionParagraphs: The MCP2515 is a stand-alone Controller Area Network (CAN) controller implementing the CAN 2.0B specification. It enables CAN connectivity for microcontrollers without built-in CAN peripherals via SPI interface.

The device features three transmit buffers, two receive buffers with acceptance filtering, and maskable interrupts for efficient message handling. Hardware acceptance filters reduce host processor load.
Operating up to 1Mbps CAN bus speed, the MCP2515 is suitable for automotive, industrial automation, and medical equipment applications requiring reliable serial communication.

faeReview.author: Mark Stevens
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Industrial Networks
faeReview.content: The MCP2515 is the industry standard for adding CAN to microcontrollers without native CAN support. The SPI interface is easy to implement, and the hardware filtering reduces CPU load significantly. I use it with Arduino, PIC, and ARM microcontrollers for CAN bus applications. Pair it with the MCP2551 transceiver for a complete CAN node.
faeReview.highlight: Industry-standard CAN controller with hardware filtering and SPI interface

faqs.question: What CAN transceiver should I use with MCP2515?
faqs.answer: The MCP2515 requires an external CAN transceiver like the MCP2551 (5V) or MCP2561 (3.3V/5V). The transceiver converts the digital CAN TX/RX signals to the differential CANH/CANL bus signals. Choose based on your supply voltage and bus speed requirements. The MCP2562 offers silent mode for power savings.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use MCP2551 for 5V systems; use MCP2561 for 3.3V or mixed voltage systems.

products.name: MCP2551 High-Speed CAN Transceiver
products.shortDescription: High-speed CAN transceiver for CAN 2.0B networks up to 1Mbps, with slope control, thermal shutdown, and protection features.
descriptionParagraphs: The MCP2551 is a high-speed CAN transceiver designed for use in CAN 2.0B networks operating at speeds up to 1Mbps. It interfaces between the CAN controller (like MCP2515) and the physical CAN bus.
The device features differential transmit and receive capability, slope control for reduced EMI, and protection features including thermal shutdown and short-circuit protection on the CAN bus pins.
The MCP2551 supports up to 112 nodes on the CAN bus and provides excellent EMC performance for harsh industrial and automotive environments.

faeReview.author: Mark Stevens
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Industrial Networks
faeReview.content: The MCP2551 is the classic CAN transceiver that pairs perfectly with the MCP2515 controller. The slope control feature is important for reducing EMI in automotive applications. I've used this combination in dozens of CAN bus projects from automotive diagnostics to industrial control systems. The protection features make it robust for harsh environments.
faeReview.highlight: Reliable CAN transceiver with slope control and protection features

faqs.question: What is the purpose of the Rs pin on MCP2551?
faqs.answer: The Rs (slope control) pin controls the slew rate of the CAN output signals. Connect Rs to ground for high-speed mode (fastest edges, highest speed). Connect a resistor (10kΩ to 100kΩ) from Rs to ground for slope control mode, which slows the edges to reduce EMI. For high-speed CAN (500k-1M), use high-speed mode. For lower speeds or reduced EMI, use slope control.
faqs.decisionGuide: Connect Rs to ground for high-speed; use 10-100kΩ resistor for slope control/EMI reduction.

products.name: ENC28J60 Stand-Alone Ethernet Controller
products.shortDescription: Stand-alone Ethernet controller with SPI interface, integrated MAC and PHY, for embedded systems requiring 10Mbps Ethernet connectivity.
descriptionParagraphs: The ENC28J60 is a stand-alone Ethernet controller with an industry-standard Serial Peripheral Interface (SPI). It is designed to serve as an Ethernet network interface for any controller equipped with SPI.

The device includes an integrated MAC and 10BASE-T PHY, 8KB transmit/receive packet buffer, and supports full and half-duplex modes. The SPI interface allows any microcontroller to add Ethernet connectivity.
With programmable automatic retransmit on collision and programmable padding and CRC generation, the ENC28J60 reduces host processor overhead for network communication.

faeReview.author: David Lee
faeReview.title: FAE - Network Connectivity
faeReview.content: The ENC28J60 is a popular choice for adding Ethernet to microcontrollers without built-in MAC. The SPI interface is simple to use, and the 8KB buffer handles complete packets without host intervention. I've used it with Arduino for web server projects and with PIC microcontrollers for industrial Ethernet applications. The integrated PHY means minimal external components - just a magjack connector.
faeReview.highlight: Complete Ethernet solution with SPI interface and integrated PHY

faqs.question: What external components are needed for ENC28J60?
faqs.answer: The ENC28J60 requires minimal external components: 1) A 25MHz crystal with loading capacitors; 2) A magnetic jack (magjack) with integrated isolation transformer; 3) Decoupling capacitors; 4) Optional status LEDs. The magjack provides the Ethernet physical interface with built-in isolation. No separate Ethernet transformer is needed.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use integrated magjack for simplest design; ensure 25MHz crystal is accurate.
